topics.title: Should social media platforms be banned for students under 16?
topics.context: Hong Kong, like many places, is debating youth access to social media. Concerns about mental health, sleep, and academic performance must be balanced against the benefits of digital citizenship and peer connection.

topics.title: Should AI writing tools be allowed in secondary school English classrooms?
topics.context: AI tools such as ChatGPT can produce polished essays in seconds. Some teachers see them as a threat to learning; others view them as the calculator of writing.

topics.title: Is daily homework necessary for academic success?
topics.context: HK students consistently rank among the world's most homework-loaded. Critics say it harms wellbeing; supporters argue it builds discipline.

topics.title: Should all secondary schools in Hong Kong adopt English as the medium of instruction?
topics.context: EMI vs CMI is a long-standing debate in Hong Kong education, balancing global competitiveness against cultural identity and learning equity.

topics.title: Should single-use plastics be completely banned in Hong Kong?
topics.context: HK rolled out partial bans recently. Environmentalists want more; small businesses warn of cost burdens.

sample_essay.topic: Should AI writing tools be allowed in secondary school English classrooms?

annotated.text: AI writing tools should be permitted in secondary school English classrooms, but only when paired with explicit instruction on argument quality.
Lam, Hew and Chiu (2018) found that Hong Kong secondary students given structured, technology-supported instruction produced stronger arguments than peers taught conventionally.
This pattern suggests that AI access alone is not the cause of improvement; structured pedagogy is.
Marzuki et al. (2023) report similar teacher-observed gains in content and organisation across EFL classrooms, lending breadth to the claim.
Although this evidence is robust for guided contexts, it may not hold where teachers lack training in AI integration.
Critics argue that any AI access erodes critical thinking; however, this position conflates the tool with how it is used, an analytical error.
